metadata.title: Assemble & Ship the Brief
metadata.playlist: Say What It Is — The Conductor Brief
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 5 brief — The Conductor Brief: all parts, assembly and submission

beats.narration_text: Hola — Liam, Episode 7 of 7. You have six sections. The question now is whether they connect. Six sharp sections that do not trace back to a common logic is not a brief — it is six answers to six different questions. The coherence pass is the step most students skip because they are tired at the end of a project. That is exactly when it matters most. This episode covers the coherence pass, the assembly format, the file naming requirement — it is specific and the wrong name costs points — and the deductions the brief lists explicitly, so you do not find out about them after submission. Then we close with where all of this goes next: Parts 1 through 6 feed five different downstream deliverables. You are not filing this in a drawer. You are building the foundation that A6 and the final pitch will stand on.

props.command: Run the coherence pass on all 6 sections, assemble into one PDF with the correct filename, and confirm you know exactly what the deductions are before you submit.
props.runningText: six sections that don't connect is not a brief — the coherence pass is the step most students skip…
output: Coherence pass: every section traces back to Part 1
File format: PDF only · filename: LastName_FirstName_A5_ConductorBrief.pdf
Where it goes next: Part 1 → interface · Part 3 → brand_config · Parts 4–5 → GIGO · Part 6 → A6 + pitch

beats.narration_text: The coherence pass has four checks. Check one: does every section trace back to Part 1? Read your Part 1 sentence. Now read your Part 2A user. Is this the exact person your Part 1 sentence describes? Read your Part 3 data sources. Does each one collect data that connects to the problem in Part 1? Read your Part 6B differentiator. Does it address the gap that your Part 1 user would care about? If any section could belong to a different product, it has drifted from the Part 1 logic. Check two: do the user stories match the user profile? The roles in the 2B stories should match the role in 2A. If 2A says email marketing coordinator and story two says data engineer, you have two different users. Pick one. Check three: does the differentiator solve the problem stated in Part 1? If Part 1 says the user can 'know what moved before the nine AM meeting' and the differentiator is about data visualization, there is a mismatch. Check four: are the phase gates specific enough to act on? Re-read one good-looks-like and one bad-looks-like. Could you make a yes-or-no decision from each? If not, tighten them before assembling.

artifactLines: Check 1: every section traces back to Part 1. Could any section belong to a different product? Fix the drift.
Check 2: user stories match the Part 2A user role. Two different roles = pick one, update both.
Check 3: Part 6B differentiator solves the problem stated in Part 1. Mismatch = rewrite one of them.
Check 4: phase gates are specific enough to make a yes/no decision. Vague gate = tighten before assembling.
The coherence test: read your Part 1 sentence to someone with no context. Now read your Part 6B. Same product?
props.sparkLine: Could any section belong to a different product? Fix the drift.

beats.narration_text: The brief must be submitted as a PDF. Not a Google Doc link. Not a Word file. Not a Notion page. PDF. The file must be named in this exact format: LastName_FirstName_A5_ConductorBrief.pdf. Capitalization matters. Underscores, not spaces. Your actual last name, then your actual first name. If you submit as Smith_John_A5_ConductorBrief.pdf, that is correct. If you submit as John Smith A5.pdf or conductorbrief.pdf, it is wrong and costs points. The brief grader reads dozens of files and the naming convention is what allows them to find and sort files quickly. Wrong filename is a deduction, not a grace-period fix. When you export to PDF: check that all six sections are present. Check that the formatting is readable — not a wall of text, not a collapsed outline. Number your sections to match the assignment brief. If the brief asks for Parts 1 through 6 and your document has sections named differently, rename them before exporting.

artifactLines: Format: PDF only. Not a Google Doc link. Not a Word file. Not a Notion page.
Filename: LastName_FirstName_A5_ConductorBrief.pdf — exact format. Underscores, not spaces.
Wrong filename = a deduction. Not a grace-period fix.
Before exporting: confirm all 6 parts are present · numbered to match the assignment brief.
After exporting: open the PDF and check it renders correctly. A corrupted export is worse than a wrong filename.
Section names should match the brief: Parts 1–6, not custom headings the grader has to map.

beats.narration_text: The brief lists three deductions explicitly. Minus 15 for any section missing — not low quality, missing. If you submit without Part 3 or Part 5, that is a 15-point deduction per section. Minus 5 for user stories in non-standard format — the grader cannot tell which slot is which, the story does not count. Minus 5 for Part 1 that describes technology instead of value — tech description, feature list, jargon, written for a developer. Now: where does this go next? Part 1 feeds the interface headline in 5A — the first thing users see. Part 3 feeds brand_config.json in 5B — the file that tells the tool what data it works with. Parts 4 and 5 feed the phase gates and GIGO layer you implement in 5B. Part 6 feeds your A6 positioning statement and your final pitch intro. You are not writing this brief for a grade and filing it away. You are writing it so that A5B, A6, and the pitch have a foundation to build on. Say what it is. Say it clearly. That is the whole assignment.

props.artifactHeading: Know the deductions before you submit
artifactLines: −15: any section missing. Not low quality — missing entirely.
−5: user stories in non-standard format. If the grader can't identify the slots, the story doesn't count.
−5: Part 1 describes technology, not value (tech description · feature list · jargon · written for a developer).
Part 1 → 5A interface headline. Part 3 → brand_config.json. Parts 4–5 → phase gates + GIGO in 5B.
Part 6 → A6 positioning statement + final pitch intro.
You are not filing this away. You are building the foundation that A5B, A6, and the pitch stand on.
props.sparkLine: Say what it is. Say it clearly. That is the whole assignment.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for the series. Six sections. One coherence thread. The coherence test is this: read your Part 1 sentence to someone who has never heard of your project. Then read your Part 6B differentiator. Do they describe the same product for the same person? If yes, you have a brief. If not, you have six answers to six different questions — go back and trace the drift. The file is LastName_FirstName_A5_ConductorBrief.pdf — exact format. Before you submit: open the PDF, confirm all six parts are present and numbered, and check that there is no section labeled 'missing' or left blank. Minus 15 per missing section. Minus 5 for non-standard stories. Minus 5 for a Part 1 that describes technology. Do not find out about these after submission.

artifactLines: Coherence test: Part 1 sentence + Part 6B differentiator → same product, same person.
File: LastName_FirstName_A5_ConductorBrief.pdf — exact filename, PDF only.
Before submit: open the PDF, all 6 parts present, numbered to match the brief.
Deductions: −15 per missing section · −5 non-standard stories · −5 Part 1 tech description.
Where it goes: Part 1 → 5A headline · Part 3 → brand_config · Parts 4–5 → GIGO · Part 6 → A6 + pitch.
You are not filing this away. This is the foundation A5B, A6, and the pitch stand on.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. One final prompt — the coherence check. Paste this into Claude with all six sections filled in. The prompt reads: Here is my complete Conductor Brief draft — all six parts. Run the coherence check: for each section, confirm that it traces back to the Part 1 sentence. Flag any section that could belong to a different product. Flag any user story that uses a role other than the one named in Part 2A. Flag any phase gate criterion that is not specific enough to make a yes-or-no decision. Flag any Part 6B differentiator where a direct competitor could match the claim in a week. After flagging, prioritize the highest-risk items and suggest one specific revision for each. Then, read my Part 1 sentence aloud to someone who has not seen the project. If they say 'what does that mean?' — that is your one revision before you export to PDF. The final read-aloud test is the same test from Episode 1. If the sentence you wrote in Week 1 still holds, everything downstream built correctly. If it doesn't, fixing it now is faster than fixing A6 later.

props.command: Here is my complete Conductor Brief draft [paste all 6 sections]. Run the coherence check: flag any section that could belong to a different product, any story with a role other than Part 2A, any phase gate too vague to make a yes/no call, any Part 6B differentiator a competitor could copy in a week. For each flag, suggest one specific revision. Then confirm: does Part 1 still pass the read-aloud test?
props.runningText: paste all 6 completed sections — the coherence check only works on the full brief, not individual sections…

beats.narration_text: Assemble and Ship the Brief. Liam, in for Bear.
